Optimisation of Cosolvent Concentration for Topical Drug Delivery Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2011;24:22–26 23 an inert, nonpenetrating and noninteractive solvent [8, 9] . More recent studies suggest that it is absorbed into the upper layers of the skin [10] . MG is composed of mixed triglycerides of fractionated C 8 –C 10 fatty acids derived from coconut oil. This compound has a wide variety of cosmetic and pharmaceutical applications [11, 12] but its effects on skin permeation are difficult to elucidate as it is typically employed in multicomponent preparations. To our knowledge, there is only one report in the litera- ture concerning the effects of medium chain triglycerides on skin permeation of a model hydrophilic drug [13] . A secondary aim of the present study was to investigate any correlation between ibuprofen transport in silicone and skin for the range of solvents studied to date. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. MO was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (UK) and MG from Sasol (Witten, Germany). The silicone mem- branes were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a mea- sured thickness of 400  m. All other materials were of reagent grade and were from VWR (Lutterworth, UK). Methods Solvent Uptake Studies Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was ob- tained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated as reported previ- ously [4] and stored in a freezer until required. The uptake of ve- hicles into silicone membrane and skin (dried for 24 h to dry weight over silica before use) was experimentally determined by a gravimetric method. The membrane was cut to an appropriate size (–1 cm 2 samples) and weighed using a balance (Sartorius, Ep- som, UK). The samples were then soaked in the vehicle for 24 h. The membranes were blotted dry with tissue paper and reweighed. Experiments were performed in triplicate, at room temperature. The amount of solvent taken up by the membrane was expressed as a weight percent. Solubility parameters for the selected vehicles were calculated using Hansen’s approach [14] . Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and an- alysed for drug content by HPLC or UV spectroscopy as described previously [4] . Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with standard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Reproducibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; the CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to the appropriate size. Permeation experiments were con- ducted with saturated solutions of ibuprofen for each vehicle us- ing Franz diffusion cells with dimensions previously reported [4] . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink conditions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experiments were performed at 32 ° C under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compart- ment with microscope cover slips. The number of replicates was at least 4. Flux values were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The perme- ability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fit- ting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [15] . 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h      d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±  (1) where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between all values were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05. Results and Discussion Solubility Table 1 illustrates the solubility of ibuprofen in MO, MG and various binary mixtures of the solvents. The higher solubility of ibuprofen in MG relative to MO ( fig. 1 ) reflects the similarity in their respective solubility parameters ( table 2 ); the calculated values are 9.4 (cal/ cm 3 ) 1/2 for MG, 7.1 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 for MO, and ibuprofen has a reported value of 9.7 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 [16] . Similar trends were observed for ibuprofen solubility in water, ethanol and PG, i.e. the closer the solubility parameter between the solute and solvent, the higher the solubility. However, with increasing amounts of MG, the solubility of ibupro- fen in MG/MO systems does not increase in the exponen- tial manner observed for ethanol/water and PG/water mixtures [4, 5] . Solvent Uptake The uptake of selected vehicles into silicone mem- brane and skin is reported in table 2 and the value for water uptake is also included for comparison. The solu- bility parameter of silicone membrane is reported as 7.5 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 [17] and the vehicles with values closest to this are taken up to a significantly (p ! 0.05) greater extent in silicone than MG and water. Optimisation of Cosolvent Concentration for Topical Drug Delivery Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2011;24:22–26 23 an inert, nonpenetrating and noninteractive solvent [8, 9] . More recent studies suggest that it is absorbed into the upper layers of the skin [10] . MG is composed of mixed triglycerides of fractionated C 8 –C 10 fatty acids derived from coconut oil. This compound has a wide variety of cosmetic and pharmaceutical applications [11, 12] but its effects on skin permeation are difficult to elucidate as it is typically employed in multicomponent preparations. To our knowledge, there is only one report in the litera- ture concerning the effects of medium chain triglycerides on skin permeation of a model hydrophilic drug [13] . A secondary aim of the present study was to investigate any correlation between ibuprofen transport in silicone and skin for the range of solvents studied to date. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. MO was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (UK) and MG from Sasol (Witten, Germany). The silicone mem- branes were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a mea- sured thickness of 400  m. All other materials were of reagent grade and were from VWR (Lutterworth, UK). Methods Solvent Uptake Studies Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was ob- tained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated as reported previ- ously [4] and stored in a freezer until required. The uptake of ve- hicles into silicone membrane and skin (dried for 24 h to dry weight over silica before use) was experimentally determined by a gravimetric method. The membrane was cut to an appropriate size (–1 cm 2 samples) and weighed using a balance (Sartorius, Ep- som, UK). The samples were then soaked in the vehicle for 24 h. The membranes were blotted dry with tissue paper and reweighed. Experiments were performed in triplicate, at room temperature. The amount of solvent taken up by the membrane was expressed as a weight percent. Solubility parameters for the selected vehicles were calculated using Hansen’s approach [14] . Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and an- alysed for drug content by HPLC or UV spectroscopy as described previously [4] . Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with standard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Reproducibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; the CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to the appropriate size. Permeation experiments were con- ducted with saturated solutions of ibuprofen for each vehicle us- ing Franz diffusion cells with dimensions previously reported [4] . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink conditions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experiments were performed at 32 ° C under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compart- ment with microscope cover slips. The number of replicates was at least 4. Flux values were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The perme- ability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fit- ting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [15] . 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h      d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±  (1) where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between all values were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05. The higher solubility of ibuprofen in MG relative to MO ( fig. 1 ) reflects the similarity in their respective solubility parameters ( table 2 ); the calculated values are 9.4 (cal/ cm 3 ) 1/2 for MG, 7.1 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 for MO, and ibuprofen has a reported value of 9.7 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 [16] . Similar trends were observed for ibuprofen solubility in water, ethanol and PG, i.e. the closer the solubility parameter between the solute and solvent, the higher the solubility. However, with increasing amounts of MG, the solubility of ibupro- fen in MG/MO systems does not increase in the exponen- tial manner observed for ethanol/water and PG/water mixtures [4, 5] . Optimisation of Cosolvent Concentration for Topical Drug Delivery Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2011;24:22–26 23 an inert, nonpenetrating and noninteractive solvent [8, 9] . More recent studies suggest that it is absorbed into the upper layers of the skin [10] . MG is composed of mixed triglycerides of fractionated C 8 –C 10 fatty acids derived from coconut oil. This compound has a wide variety of cosmetic and pharmaceutical applications [11, 12] but its effects on skin permeation are difficult to elucidate as it is typically employed in multicomponent preparations. To our knowledge, there is only one report in the litera- ture concerning the effects of medium chain triglycerides on skin permeation of a model hydrophilic drug [13] . A secondary aim of the present study was to investigate any correlation between ibuprofen transport in silicone and skin for the range of solvents studied to date. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. MO was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (UK) and MG from Sasol (Witten, Germany). The silicone mem- branes were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a mea- sured thickness of 400  m. All other materials were of reagent grade and were from VWR (Lutterworth, UK). Methods Solvent Uptake Studies Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was ob- tained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated as reported previ- ously [4] and stored in a freezer until required. The uptake of ve- hicles into silicone membrane and skin (dried for 24 h to dry weight over silica before use) was experimentally determined by a gravimetric method. The membrane was cut to an appropriate size (–1 cm 2 samples) and weighed using a balance (Sartorius, Ep- som, UK). The samples were then soaked in the vehicle for 24 h. The membranes were blotted dry with tissue paper and reweighed. Experiments were performed in triplicate, at room temperature. The amount of solvent taken up by the membrane was expressed as a weight percent. Solubility parameters for the selected vehicles were calculated using Hansen’s approach [14] . Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and an- alysed for drug content by HPLC or UV spectroscopy as described previously [4] . Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with standard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Reproducibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; the CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to the appropriate size. Permeation experiments were con- ducted with saturated solutions of ibuprofen for each vehicle us- ing Franz diffusion cells with dimensions previously reported [4] . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink conditions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experiments were performed at 32 ° C under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compart- ment with microscope cover slips. The number of replicates was at least 4. Flux values were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The perme- ability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fit- ting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [15] . 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h      d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±  (1) where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between all values were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05. Results and Discussion Solubility Table 1 illustrates the solubility of ibuprofen in MO, MG and various binary mixtures of the solvents. The higher solubility of ibuprofen in MG relative to MO ( fig. 1 ) reflects the similarity in their respective solubility parameters ( table 2 ); the calculated values are 9.4 (cal/ cm 3 ) 1/2 for MG, 7.1 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 for MO, and ibuprofen has a reported value of 9.7 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 [16] . Similar trends were observed for ibuprofen solubility in water, ethanol and PG, i.e. the closer the solubility parameter between the solute and solvent, the higher the solubility. However, with increasing amounts of MG, the solubility of ibupro- fen in MG/MO systems does not increase in the exponen- tial manner observed for ethanol/water and PG/water mixtures [4, 5] . Solvent Uptake The uptake of selected vehicles into silicone mem- brane and skin is reported in table 2 and the value for water uptake is also included for comparison. The solu- bility parameter of silicone membrane is reported as 7.5 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 [17] and the vehicles with values closest to this are taken up to a significantly (p ! 0.05) greater extent in silicone than MG and water.
